Usage Guide

Creator ResourcesFree Mystery YouTube Scripts
Scripts Home/The Rake Creepypasta YouTube Script

Internet Folklore Creator Library

The Rake Creepypasta YouTube Script

The Rake Creepypasta is prepared as a creator-ready production workspace based on the archive record, with long-form scenes, Shorts narration, image prompts, music keywords, and editing notes.

Genre
Internet Folklore Creator Library
Estimated video length
5-6 minutes
Longform script included
Yes
Shorts script included
Yes
Image prompts included
Yes
Thumbnail ideas included
Yes

Story Summary

The Rake is treated through one concrete anchor: the internet horror figure described as a pale humanoid creature that appears in bedrooms, woods, and fragmented online accounts.

The Rake Creepypasta: How a Pale Figure Became Internet Folklore works as a video because it gives the creator a clear subject, a recognizable setting, and a central motif around internet folklore, creepypasta creature. The production should help viewers understand the main event, the background, and the emotional shape of the story before moving into platform-specific execution.

Digital folklore and liminal-space setting should be presented with a digital, liminal, and unsettling tone. Use the original archive record as the source reference, but keep factual claims, legendary motifs, and interpretive atmosphere clearly separated.

Story Information

Genre
Internet Folklore Creator Library
Core Motif
internet folklore, creepypasta creature
Main Subject
The Rake Creepypasta: How a Pale Figure Became Internet Folklore
Setting
Digital folklore and liminal-space setting
Mood
digital, liminal, and unsettling
Recommended Video Length
5-6 minutes
Difficulty
Beginner-friendly production package

Creator Toolkit

Images

Create one clear visual for each Scene with any image tool you prefer.

Example: GPTExample: MidjourneyExample: FluxExample: SDXL

Narration

Turn the Scene narration into a clear voice track using AI voice or a natural recording.

Example: AI narrationExample: text to speechExample: natural AI voiceExample: voice over

Background Music

Choose quiet music that supports the mood without covering the narration.

Example: Dark AmbientExample: Cinematic DroneExample: Mystery AtmosphereExample: Low Drone

Editing

Assemble the visuals, voice, and music in Scene order with your preferred editor.

Example: CapCutExample: DaVinci ResolveExample: Premiere ProExample: video editor

Motion (Optional)

Optional motion can add gentle movement after the main Scene image is ready.

Example: Motion PromptExample: image to videoExample: slow push-inExample: subtle camera motion

Production Workflow

  1. ①
    Story

    Read the Story Summary.

  2. ②
    Format

    Choose Long-form or Short-form.

  3. ③
    Narration

    Prepare the Scene narration.

  4. ④
    Image

    Create images from the Image Prompts.

  5. ⑤
    Music

    Choose music from the mood keywords.

  6. ⑥
    Voice

    Prepare the voice track.

  7. ⑦
    Edit

    Place the Scenes in order.

  8. ⑧
    Finish

    Export the video.

Small production note

You do not need to make the first version perfect. Finishing the first video from beginning to end matters most.

Long-form Creator

Scene 1

Scene Role: Initial Appearance

Estimated Playback Time: Stored runtime: 5 min 40 sec (340 sec final) / 4 min 54 sec (294 sec narration)

Narration: The account identifies the central subject clearly: The Rake Creepypasta: How a Pale Figure Became Internet Folklore. The action moves through a specific event: The effect depends on vagueness. The conflict begins with a specific pressure: The figure matters because it shows how online communities can build folklore from uncertainty. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: The Rake uses that style to make fiction feel like something assembled from damaged evidence. The opening identity is already specific: The Rake centers on internet Folklore. The account stays careful because the evidence has boundaries: The Rake appears in source material connected with creepypasta history, forum-era horror writing, meme documentation, digital folklore, and repeated online retellings. The account stays careful because the evidence has boundaries: References such as Know Your Meme and Creepypasta Wiki are used as orientation points, not as proof for every later claim.

Narration Part 1

Narration: The account identifies the central subject clearly: The Rake Creepypasta: How a Pale Figure Became Internet Folklore. The action moves through a specific event: The effect depends on vagueness. The conflict begins with a specific pressure: The figure matters because it shows how online communities can build folklore from uncertainty. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: The Rake uses that style to make fiction feel like something assembled from damaged evidence.

Estimated Reading Time: ≈ 31 sec

Creator Note: The Rake must stay tied to the effect depends on vagueness. Do not add figures, motives, or incidents beyond the figure matters because it shows how online communities can build folklore from uncertainty.

Visual Beats:

Image Prompt 1: A realistic digital-documentary style image shows The Rake connected to the effect depends on vagueness in creepypasta history, forum-era horror writing, meme documentation, digital folklore, and repeated online retellings. Frame one clear scene as a wide establishing composition with the main subject easy to identify, with dim screen-adjacent light, digital mystery, and cultural context from Internet Folklore. Include effect, depends, vagueness as supporting visual details. This should feel like an opening visual with one clear focal point during the first sequence. No gore, logos, watermarks, unrelated fantasy elements, or readable fake interface text.

Beat Motion: Use a slow controlled push toward The Rake, with connected to the effect depends on vagueness staying fixed as the opening anchor and effect visible. Use only light shifts that match the internet folklore tone.

Image Prompt 2: A realistic digital-documentary style image shows The Rake held as the central visual in creepypasta history, forum-era horror writing, meme documentation, digital folklore, and repeated online retellings. Frame one clear scene as a medium composition that shows the important action or state, with dim screen-adjacent light, digital mystery, and cultural context from Internet. Include The Rake, The Rake Creepypasta, creepypasta as supporting visual details. This should feel like an opening visual that adds a second visual detail during the first sequence. No gore, logos, watermarks, unrelated fantasy elements, or readable fake interface text.

Beat Motion: Use a restrained lateral pan across creepypasta history, forum-era horror writing, meme documentation, digital folklore, and repeated online retellings, letting The Rake hold the opening detail while The Rake Creepypasta stays in frame. Use only light shifts that match the internet folklore tone.

Image Prompt 3: A realistic digital-documentary style image shows The Rake held as the central visual in creepypasta history, forum-era horror writing, meme documentation, digital folklore, and repeated online retellings. Frame one clear scene as a close visual detail that supports the current narration, with dim screen-adjacent light, digital mystery, and cultural context from internet. Include The Rake, internet, Folklore as supporting visual details. This should feel like an opening visual that widens the frame without adding new story elements during the first sequence. No gore, logos, watermarks, unrelated fantasy elements, or readable fake interface text.

Beat Motion: Start with a static hold, then pull back slightly to reveal held as the central visual and Folklore within the wider frame. Use only light shifts that match the internet folklore tone.

Scene 2

Scene Role: Spread and Core Clues

Estimated Playback Time: Stored runtime: 5 min 40 sec (340 sec final) / 4 min 54 sec (294 sec narration)

Narration: The image is grounded in a specific object or setting: Some versions make The Rake a bedroom intruder. The action moves through a specific event: The story feels archived, but the archive itself is part of the fiction. The account then gives a direct movement: The creature is usually described as thin, pale, and disturbingly human without being fully human. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: Images and fan versions also changed the creature, giving it a more fixed visual identity than the early text alone required. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: The Rake is treated through creepypasta history, forum-era horror writing, meme documentation, digital folklore, and repeated online retellings. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: For The Rake, early online form, reposted versions, and later community additions are not collapsed into one origin.

Scene 3

Scene Role: Key Development or Later Additions

Estimated Playback Time: Stored runtime: 5 min 40 sec (340 sec final) / 4 min 54 sec (294 sec narration)

Narration: The story changes course through this action: Internet horror figure described as a pale humanoid creature that appears in bedrooms, woods, and fragmented online accounts. The tradition allows more than one form of the account: Some versions make The Rake a bedroom intruder. The tradition allows more than one form of the account: Others connect it to older reports, forest sightings, or hidden documents. The retelling changes when variant details appear: Images and fan versions also changed the creature, giving it a more fixed visual identity than the early text alone required. The account then gives a direct movement: The Rake takes shape through a repeatable online image: the internet horror figure described as a pale humanoid creature that appears in bedrooms, woods.

Narration Part 1

Scene 4

Scene Role: Evidence, Attribution, and Uncertainty

Estimated Playback Time: Stored runtime: 5 min 40 sec (340 sec final) / 4 min 54 sec (294 sec narration)

Narration: The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: Later retellings do not always keep the same emphasis. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: Public references used for orientation include Know Your Meme and Creepypasta Wiki. The surviving record gives the narration a source limit: Those references are useful for following regional tradition, collected folklore, and repeated motifs. Original archive story

Read the original archive story.

The Rake Creepypasta: How a Pale Figure Became Internet Folklore

Reference

Use this page as a production workspace for a video based on the archive entry for The Rake Creepypasta. Keep source limits clear and avoid presenting later interpretations as verified facts.
